common_name: Philippine Ground Orchid (Large Purple Ground Orchid, Tall Spathoglottis)

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs or container-grown clumps in spring in partial shade in well-drained, humus-rich, orchid bark-enriched soil; Spathoglottis plicata is the most garden-adaptable of the ground orchids, tolerating a much wider range of soil and light conditions than epiphytic orchids. In zone 9-10, established clumps may survive mild winters with mulch protection — in zone 9a, treat as a perennial with some dieback risk in freeze years. Blooms repeatedly through the warm months. Divide clumps every 3–4 years. An excellent beginner-friendly orchid for outdoor subtropical gardens. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Plant in partial shade to bright indirect light in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil — Philippine Ground Orchid performs best in the filtered light and moist, organically rich conditions of a shaded garden bed and will produce fewer flowers and show leaf stress in intense full sun. Spathoglottis plicata is a clump-forming terrestrial orchid native to a vast natural range from the Philippines, Malaysia, and Indonesia through the Pacific Islands to northern Australia and New Caledonia in the Orchidaceae family — one of the easiest-to-grow orchid species for outdoor gardens in zone 9-12 because, unlike most commonly grown orchids, it is truly terrestrial: it grows in ordinary garden soil rather than requiring bark mounts or special epiphyte media. The plant produces oval to egg-shaped pseudobulbs that store water, topped with 2–4 narrow, pleated (the 'plicata' — folded), bright green leaves 12–24 inches long. From the pseudobulbs emerge tall flower scapes (18–36 inches) bearing 6–20 flowers in succession — each flower a typical orchid shape (3 sepals, 2 petals, 1 lip) in shades of deep rose-pink to magenta-purple with a yellow throat and slightly marked lip. The plant blooms repeatedly through spring, summer, and fall in zone 9-10, with individual scapes blooming for several weeks before a new scape emerges. An outstanding and underused orchid for zone 9-11 shade and partial-shade garden beds. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Spathoglottis plicata (Ground Orchid) for Florida
LSU AgCenter — Ground Orchids for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 9-10
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Spathoglottis plicata for Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens
American Orchid Society — Spathoglottis plicata cultivation guide

common_name: Clove Vine (Cipo Cravo, Cipó Cravo, Panama Clove Vine)

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in late spring on a strong trellis, tree, or arbor in full sun to partial shade; Tynanthus panurensis is frost-sensitive and suited to zone 10-11 outdoor culture. The vine climbs vigorously and its bark, when scraped or cut, releases an immediate, powerful clove fragrance — the same eugenol compound found in true cloves (Syzygium aromaticum). In traditional Amazonian herbal medicine, the bark is macerated in rum or cachaça to create a tonic ('licor de cipó cravo'). Grows in zone 9 as a container plant moved indoors in winter. Propagate from cuttings.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil with strong climbing support — Clove Vine grows vigorously and its stems will clothe a fence, trellis, or tree in dense, attractive foliage once established. Tynanthus panurensis is a woody, liana-climbing vine native to the Amazonian regions of Peru, Brazil, Bolivia, Colombia, and Ecuador in the Bignoniaceae family, known not primarily for its flowers (small, tubular, cream to white, produced in axillary clusters) but for one of the most remarkable aromatic properties of any tropical climbing plant: the bark, stems, and wood of the vine smell powerfully, precisely, and unmistakably of cloves — the familiar spice from Syzygium aromaticum — when cut, scraped, or bruised. The fragrant compound responsible is eugenol, the same aromatic phenol that gives true cloves their characteristic fragrance, occurring in Tynanthus panurensis as a convergent natural chemistry that produces an olfactory experience nearly identical to the spice. Throughout the Amazon Basin, the bark of T. panurensis is harvested and macerated in spirits (rum, cachaça, or aguardiente) to create 'licor de cipó cravo' (clove vine liqueur) — a traditional tonic drink used medicinally and consumed recreationally with an unmistakable clove-spice flavor. A genuinely unique aromatic plant for zone 10-12 tropical garden enthusiasts interested in useful and ethnobotanically significant plants.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden — Tynanthus panurensis species profile
Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute — Amazonian medicinal lianas: Tynanthus
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tropical Bignoniaceae vines for South Florida
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Unusual tropical vines for zone 10-12

common_name: Magic Flowers (Hot Water Plant, Cupid's Bower, Achimenes, Orchid Pansy)

planting.note: Start scaly rhizomes (caterpillar-like propagules) indoors in shallow trays of moist peat-perlite mix in late winter; the common name 'hot water plant' comes from the old practice of watering with warm water to stimulate sprouting. Plant in hanging baskets or shade garden beds in partial to full shade once temperatures are consistently above 60°F. Achimenes goes dormant in fall — allow plants to dry and store the tiny rhizomes at 50–60°F through winter. In zone 9, rhizomes left in ground with heavy mulch may survive mild winters. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in well-draining, humus-rich, slightly acidic potting mix — Magic Flowers performs best in the bright, indirect or filtered light of a sheltered shade garden or covered porch and will scorch in direct summer sun. Achimenes hybrida encompasses the large group of garden hybrid achimenes (family Gesneriaceae) developed from crosses among approximately 25 wild species native to Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, and other parts of Central America — a genus valued as one of the finest shade-garden summer-blooming plants available. The plants grow from tiny, caterpillar-shaped scaly rhizomes (the 'scales' are actually modified leaves with overlapping bracts) that sprout readily when planted in warm conditions. The resulting plants produce trailing to semi-upright stems of soft, dark-green, hairy, opposite leaves covered in a continuous flush of flowers from early summer through fall — each individual flower a flat-faced, broadly funnel-shaped bloom 1–2 inches across in the full color range: white, pink, rose, magenta, purple, lavender, red, and bicolors. The prolific, non-stop flowering habit in the shade — a position where many other summer flowers fail — is the defining advantage of Achimenes hybrida. Excellent in hanging baskets and window boxes in a shaded location, where the trailing stems create a summer-long cascade of color. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Achimenes for Florida shade gardens
American Gesneriad Society — Achimenes hybrida cultivation and species guide
LSU AgCenter — Shade-Loving Summer Bulbs for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Achimenes for zone 9 shade gardens

common_name: Cape Primrose (Nodding Violet, Trailing Cape Primrose, Saxicolous Streptocarpus)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light in very well-draining, peat-based or coir-based potting mix; Streptocarpus saxorum is particularly susceptible to root rot — use a fast-draining mix and allow the surface to dry slightly between waterings. It thrives in hanging baskets or cascading over the edge of containers. Prefers moderate temperatures (60–80°F) and avoids extreme summer heat. Blooms nearly continuously in good conditions. Propagate from leaf cuttings or stem tip cuttings. Excellent on a bright, cool, shaded porch in zone 9 from spring through fall, with winter indoors.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in well-draining, porous potting mix — Cape Primrose produces its best bloom in bright indirect light and will fail in dim positions while being scorched by any direct sun exposure. Streptocarpus saxorum is a compact, trailing to semi-prostrate perennial native to Tanzania (and the broader East African highlands) in the Gesneriaceae family, one of approximately 155 Streptocarpus species and one of the most delicate and graceful of the trailing gesneriads for container and hanging basket culture. The plant produces trailing stems of small (0.75–1 inch), round, velvety, mid-green leaves with a softly hairy texture. From among the leaves, delicate, thin flower stalks emerge throughout the growing season carrying one or two pale lavender to soft lilac flowers at the tip — each flower a small (1-inch), tubular, slightly two-lipped bloom with a white throat and five spreading rounded lobes, borne on long, arching pedicels that nod gracefully in the slightest air movement. The overall effect of multiple cascading stems with dozens of nodding lavender flowers is charming, elegant, and refined. S. saxorum's relatively compact form, continuous bloom habit, and tolerance of lower light levels than many flowering plants make it an excellent choice for shaded porches, indoor bright-window positions, and cool, sheltered shade gardens in warm climates. Often grown as a houseplant at the boundary between indoor and outdoor culture. Non-toxic.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Streptocarpus saxorum cultivation
American Gesneriad Society — Streptocarpus saxorum species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Gesneriads for Florida indoor and shade gardens
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Streptocarpus saxorum

common_name: Browne's Savory (Swamp Savory, Florida Pennyroyal, Creeping Savory)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in full sun to partial shade in moist to wet, well-drained to seasonally flooded soil; Clinopodium brownei is a native wetland and moist-habitat herb that thrives at pond margins, in rain gardens, and in moist Florida/Gulf Coast garden beds where many other herbs would drown. In zone 9, it grows as a reliable perennial in moist areas, spreading by stolons. The aromatic foliage has a pleasant pennyroyal/savory fragrance when crushed. Excellent groundcover for consistently moist to seasonally wet areas. Attractive to bees and pollinators. Native plant of high ecological value.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in consistently moist to wet soil — Browne's Savory is uniquely among aromatic herbs in tolerating and preferring consistently moist, even seasonally wet, conditions that would rot most culinary herbs. Clinopodium brownei is a low-growing, mat-forming, rhizomatous aromatic herb native to the coastal plain of the southeastern United States (peninsular Florida north through coastal Georgia), the Caribbean islands, Mexico, and Central and South America in the Lamiaceae (mint family). The plant grows as a trailing, spreading groundcover with small, oval to round, bright-green, somewhat succulent leaves that, when brushed or crushed, release a pleasant, distinctly pennyroyal-like to savory-like aroma — a clean, slightly minty, herbal fragrance that makes Browne's Savory attractive as a groundcover in garden areas where foot traffic will release the scent. Tiny, pale lavender to white, two-lipped flowers are produced in clusters throughout the warm months, attractive to native bees and small pollinators. The plant spreads by stolons (horizontal runners) to form a low, dense, aromatic groundcover over moist soil areas. Browne's Savory is a native Florida plant of high ecological value — it serves as a larval host plant for the Florida-native hairy-footed flower bee and supports numerous native pollinator species. Excellent for rain gardens, pond margins, and consistently moist landscape areas in zone 8-11 where a fragrant, low-growing native groundcover is needed.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Clinopodium brownei (Browne's Savory) native plant
Florida Native Plant Society — Clinopodium brownei species profile
LSU AgCenter — Native groundcovers for Gulf Coast Louisiana moist gardens
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Clinopodium brownei
